By Shawn Twing
In the previous lesson I said that Google search is all about active
intent. Facebook, on the other hand, is about passive potential
interest.
Using a variety of factors — age, gender, geography, urban vs. rural,
estimated income, job title, and especially interests — we’re able to
advertise to audiences that aren’t actively looking for what we
have to offer but who might, after learning about it, be
interested.
Let all of that sink in … Facebook users are NOT looking for
what you have to offer (a solution for their problem).
In fact, your ad might be an intrusion into their personal space,
showing up between pictures of their kids and the latest post
from their best friend.
“Straight to Offer” Approach
That’s why conventional approaches to marketing on Facebook
The Info-Tainment Mindset
(Advertising on Facebook)
www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 18 --

often underperform or fail completely.
I usually group all of these together into one category that I call
“straight to offer”. The offer can be just about anything — a PDF
lead magnet, four-part video series, webinar, etc. The basic
strategy is the same — click here and get the prize so I can try to
sell you immediately or follow up with you forever.
Facebook tolerates straight to offer, but that tolerance usually
comes with a price in the form of higher click costs.
Let me be clear that ads that lead to offers on Facebook are
perfectly OK (in fact, we’re going to use a lot of those later) — but
they’re not the best ENTRY POINT into a relationship with a
prospect.
Perhaps the worst part of direct to offer ads is that we force the
prospect into a binary choice — yes or no, opt in or not, sign up or
not, download or not.
And guess what?
Most prospects choose “not.” Then we’re left with making another
yes/no offer — “You didn’t like that, so how about this?” And on and on
it goes.
“Straight to Argument” Approach
www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 18 --

What’s the alternative?
It’s something I call “straight to argument.”
I don’t mean that to suggest a disagreement (although you could
certainly position it that way to establish some intrigue).
Instead, when I say “straight to argument” I mean that I am
beginning a relationship with a prospect by sharing something I
believe will be interesting and valuable to my perfect prospect
(but not others).
That could be an opinion, an idea, a way of seeing the world, the
hint of a counterintuitive secret — the critical ingredient is that it
should appeal to the right people and be easily ignored by others.
More importantly, all I’m asking for in the beginning is their
attention. That’s the signal I want to see to know I’m on the right
track.
But here’s the challenge — it’s really easy to get clicks on Facebook.
A great ad with a great headline can do that all day long, especially
if the headline uses some of the tried and true clickbait-style
headlines.
That means I need to lean in the opposite direction — making
my ad headlines, imagery, and copy appeal very narrowly only
to the audience I’m most interested in.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 18 --

For example, I worked with a client who trained sales managers to
get better performance from their sales teams. It’s easy to target
sales professionals on Facebook, and it’s easy to get their attention
in the forms of clicks:
▪ “Top 10 Ways to Close the Sale” video series,
▪ “5 Books Every Sales Professional Should Read,”
▪ etc.
However, this client only wanted the attention of sales managers
which meant that our ad creative and MPPS copy needed to
appeal ONLY to sales managers, not to sales people in general.
That’s a completely different way of thinking about your
audience.
Our focus couldn’t be on anything that was generally
interesting/appealing to sales professional.
So the SOI we created and the ad copy that supported it focused
entirely on the value of sales team training, knowing that was
narrowly interesting to our intended audience, but not
compelling to other sales professionals.
As I mentioned before — we need to make sure we pre-qualify our
audience careful.
We’ll talk about how to determine if we’re successful �ltering
www.getwsodo.com

-- 4 of 18 --

out/in later in lesson seven, but for right now I want to introduce
an idea to help you understand how I use this strategy day in and
day out.
Deciding on a Meaningful Signal to Optimize
On
Facebook has options for many different types of optimization.
This allows you, as the advertiser, to tell Facebook the signal that’s
relevant to your business, and then bene�t from Facebook’s big
data and AI to optimize for that signal.
For example, if you have a lead-generation campaign that is
optimized for leads, you would de�ne an audience that you think
is relevant, bid and optimize for leads, and as leads are generated
Facebook will look at the accumulating data and use its algorithm
and analysis to improve your results.
The more data you give Facebook to work with (usually 25-100
conversions) the more effectively Facebook optimizes on your
behalf.
But you MUST make sure you’ve selected a meaningful signal
to represent what counts as a lead.
This is critical. If you targeted a very speci�c audience and offered
a very general lead magnet in exchange for an opt in, that
www.getwsodo.com

-- 5 of 18 --

wouldn’t be a particularly meaningful signal.
Here’s how you can use this for your SOI-inspired multi-page
presell site.
Instead of optimizing for leads, the signal you optimize for are
people who see page-two of your MPPS content.
Think about the quality of that signal for a moment.
Let’s assume you have 1,000 words of content on page one of your
MPPS and you open a couple of loops that you’ll close later on
pages 2, 3, and 4. You send 100 people to that page and no one
clicks past page one.
That’s a very clear signal there’s a mismatch between the
audience and the idea. You may have had a great ad that got clicks,
but you didn’t get people to engage with your content past page
one.
Now imagine a scenario where you send 100 people to page one of
your MPPS and 75% of them click through to page two. That’s a
very strong positive signal!
When we �rst send traf�c to an MPPS from Facebook, we
optimize for page two views because that’s meaningful to validate
the match between audience and message.
That also gives Facebook a valuable, accurate signal to guide its
www.getwsodo.com

-- 6 of 18 --

optimization efforts on our behalf.
I recommend that you validate a new audience (or an existing
audience and a new MPPS) using this page one to page two
conversion metric.
Over time you’ll optimize for different factors depending on the
length and complexity of your MPPS. In my experience, this is the
most impactful method for verifying and optimizing Facebook
audiences who you’re sending to an MPPS.
Brainstorming Your Initial POP Segments
In a previous lesson I mentioned the grid format we use for
de�ning audiences.
The grid itself is less important than the way you use it to think
about how you’re de�ning an audience. The most important thing
to understand here is that you need to identify what’s important
in distinguishing your particular pocket of people.
▪ Who are they? Male, female, how old?
▪ Where are they? Country, state/region, urban vs. rural?
▪ Do they have job titles or other indicators that are important in
your targeting?
▪ And �nally, what interests do they have that might indicate they’re
in your POP?
www.getwsodo.com

-- 7 of 18 --

In my business we call this “baking the cupcake.”
The idea is to identify the list of ingredients that, taken
together, suggest someone is in your POP, and to use those factors
to learn more about which ones have the most impact on that
audience’s view of your business.
There’s another really important reason to do this — if you know
who you have targeted, you know who you’re talking to in your ad
copy/creative!
It’s also critically important to segment your audiences in
ways that are meaningful.
At �rst that may be to test/validate audiences, but over time it’s
www.getwsodo.com

-- 8 of 18 --

also a way to understand how different audience segments are
monetized by your particular business model.
Referring back to principle #1 in the �rst lesson, the place to begin
thinking about how to segment your audience is to start with
perfect.
▪ If you have an existing business, who is your ideal prospect? Male
or female?
▪ Approximately how old (5-10 year ranges are OK here)?
▪ If your business has a geographic location (for example, if you
run live events), might it improve performance if you targeted an
area where people who see your ad could drive to the location easily?
▪ Does your ideal prospect have a speci�c job title?
▪ Or is s/he in a speci�c industry?
▪ Are there any thought leaders in that industry that your ideal
prospect might follow online?
▪ Are there other interests that are common to your ideal prospects
— personal or professional?
▪ Have you noticed that your prospects read the same books,
frequent the same websites, have the same hobbies?
Remember, when we’re creating the perfect audience we’re
looking for the factors that provide the strongest positive signal
that someone might be an ideal prospect.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 9 of 18 --

If you’re not sure, keep a list of ideas and only include the factors
you believe are most relevant for your perfect audience. You can
add audience segments later and compare those to your imagined
ideal.
Let’s look at an example of a business that helps alternative health
professionals (chiropractors, massage therapists, craniosacral
practitioners, etc.) get more clients and make more money.
Their business model is an annual training program delivered
online monthly and in-person quarterly, and they acquire new
clients with a value-focused weekend training event that sets up
the sale for the annual program on Sunday afternoon.
Immediately we know there are professional interests that de�ne
our audience — chiropractors, massage therapists, etc.
We also know that geography is a factor — clients attend
quarterly events in-person as part of their annual training.
Age also matters.
It’s not perfect, but in general prospects who have been working
in their �eld for a while and haven’t produced the results they
want are more likely to become clients than prospects who are
just starting out.
Gender doesn’t matter from a conversion perspective, but it’s still
a segment we’ll use to match the visuals to our audience (for
www.getwsodo.com

-- 10 of 18 --

example, ads being shown to women would have images of
women and ads being show to men would have images of men).
When we put all of this information into our targeting grid
format we start with one ideal segment (divided into two
audiences by gender).
We then decide we want to go one step further and speak directly
to the prospect’s practice/discipline, so we then segment our ideal
audience into chiropractors, massage therapists, and
craniosacral practitioners.
(This also helps make our imagery and messaging even more
representative of the target audience.)
Our perfect audience then looks like this:
▪ Segment #1: Male, 30-45, within 250 miles of the company’s
location, chiropractor
▪ Segment #2: Female, 30-45, within 250 miles of the company’s
location, chiropractor
▪ Segment #3: Male, 30-45, within 250 miles of the company’s
location, massage therapist
▪ Segment #4: Female, 30-45, within 250 miles of the company’s
location, massage therapist
▪ Segment #5: Male, 30-45, within 250 miles of the company’s
location, craniosacral practitioner
www.getwsodo.com

-- 11 of 18 --

▪ Segment #6: Female, 30-45, within 250 miles of the company’s
location, craniosacral practitioner
Each of these segments would have its own imagery and
messaging as a congruent entry point into a multi-page
presell site.
The differences in each segment are subtle, but we’re
hypothesizing that they might affect performance.
As we begin to send traf�c from these audiences to our MPPS,
we’re able to evaluate performance by segment.
At �rst we’re curious about the high-level differences — cost per
click, page one to page two conversion, and average page
depth/content consumption.
Later we’ll calculate opt in rates, cost per lead, and value per lead
by segment. This data builds our knowledge and understanding
of our audience and their relative performance.
We might learn, for example, that a craniosacral practitioner is
2x more expensive to acquire yet 5x more valuable (or 2x more
expensive and one-half as valuable!).
We’ll get into more details about measuring MPPS performance
for paid traf�c in lesson seven.
For now I want you to think about the meaningful distinctions in
www.getwsodo.com

-- 12 of 18 --

your existing or imagined audience, and how you might segment
those audiences in ways that would give you actionable
information.
Actionable is the key word.
We don’t care about TBU information (true, but useless).
We want to make distinctions that lead to action now and in the
future. In the example above, the �rst action I can take by
segmenting the audience the way I did would be using different
creative (especially imagery) in my Facebook ads.
That could be something as simple as showing an image of a man
in a white doctor’s coat for the male chiropractor audience.
I can make that more complex — for example, referring
speci�cally to an issue I know that chiropractors have generating
clients (or whatever my MPPS communicates).
As data is available, I can also act on that knowledge immediately.
If I were to �nd, for example, that massage therapists cost half as
much per click and convert to second page visitors at 10x
everyone else, I might decide to focus most, or all, of my budget
on that audience (and possibly change the copy of my MPPS to
speak speci�cally to that audience).
The opportunities to bene�t from this information are endless
www.getwsodo.com

-- 13 of 18 --

and the time required to setup an account this way is minimal.
Getting the Best From Facebook
OK, now that we know how to brainstorm and segment an
audience, let’s talk about speci�c ways we can get the best
performance from Facebook.
Rule #1: Lead With Value
Rule #1 is to leave the person a little better off then when you
found them — lead with value.
What do I mean by that?
We’re not in the business of tricking people into clicking on our
ads, or tricking them into buying whatever we’re selling. We’re in
the business of identifying and exchanging value with an
audience.
André and I have a similar appreciation and affection for Jay
Abraham’s Strategy of Preeminence and it’s a perfect lens through
which to view your Facebook advertising.
The questions I want you to ask and answer are:
▪ How, speci�cally, am I adding value to the person who clicks on
a Facebook ad and sees page one of my MPPS?
www.getwsodo.com

-- 14 of 18 --

▪ Have I given them a new perspective to see a challenge they’ve
been struggling with?
▪ Did I share something I’ve learned that I have found valuable
and believe they might bene�t from too?
▪ Have I used my expertise to make something that seems
complex easier to understand?
I want you to take this VERY seriously.
Remember, your ad is in a person’s personal space.
They weren’t looking for you, and they didn’t ask for your ad to
show up.
In the best case scenario you’re an intrusion — the last thing we
want to do is be an annoyance too. The way we prevent that, and
turn the situation in our favor, is by turning the situation in our
prospect’s favor.
The questions I ask myself when brainstorming ad creating and
landing page content begin with “what is a problem my prospect
has, right now, that I could easily solve?”
There are an in�nite number of problems large and small. You
don’t need to solve world hunger with a Facebook ad, but the
mindset should be the same.
A real person is seeing my ad, s/he is seeing that ad in personal
www.getwsodo.com

-- 15 of 18 --

space, and my most important priority is to make sure I’ve left a
positive impression on the person seeing my ad.
Rule #2: Lead With Empathy, Not
With Fear
We all know fear motivates and fear sells.
However, Facebook HATES fear-based marketing.
You won’t see that in any of�cial documentation, but it’s a
consistent message from Facebook reps and account feedback
from Facebook (click pricing, ad disapprovals, etc.).
Empathy, on the other hand, is a way to lead with value while
acknowledging that your prospect’s world might not be 100%
sunshine and unicorns.
It’s perfectly OK to recognize a problem that exists if you’re
pointing in the direction of a solution — but we have to be careful
not to ring the fear bell too much and too often.
My focusing question often is, “Would I say this to a stranger on a
plane?”
If you knew you had just sat down next to someone you’ve
never met, and that person is an ideal prospect for your
www.getwsodo.com

-- 16 of 18 --

business, would you blurt out your ad copy?
This may sound ridiculous, but I encourage you to try this little
experiment — imagine the stranger on a plane scenario, really
picture that person in your mind, imagine you’re sitting right next
to them (6″ away in coach), and now read your ad copy out loud.
How does that make your feel? How do you think it made that
person feel?
Before we move on to lesson 6 — Facebook ads to your MPPS are
the BEGINNING of the process.
They set the tone.
They also initiate the process where we will use use behavioral
signals from our audience to continually re-engage with them.
Let that knowledge take some of the pressure off of trying to
convert someone right away. By its nature Facebook traf�c is a
less-engaged audience than other forms of traf�c. That’s reality.
We’re designing a system that’ll compensate for that, so go
overboard in the beginning to make a good �rst impression,
deliver real value, and establish yourself as a bright spot among all
the noise on Facebook.
That’ll make everything that follows in the next lesson even more
effective.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 17 of 18 --

www.getwsodo.com

-- 18 of 18 --
